title: from coolie drink to cool again the toddy revival
full_text: Main Eat Drink Reviews Insider At Home About Us Download Our Magazine Q2 2026 Q1 2026 Vol 1 Palate Dining Guide 2025-2026 Main Eat Drink Reviews Insider At Home About Us Download Our Magazine Q2 2026 Q1 2026 Vol 1 Palate Dining Guide 2025-2026 Now Reading: From Coolie Drink To Cool Again: The Toddy Revival 1 01 From Coolie Drink To Cool Again: The Toddy Revival Main// Eat// Drink// Reviews// Insider// At Home// About Us// Download Our Magazine// Q2 2026// Q1 2026// Vol 1 Palate Dining Guide 2025-2026// Home Drink From Coolie Drink To Cool Again: The Toddy RevivalFrom Coolie Drink To Cool Again: The Toddy RevivalColin GomezDrink9 months agoShare Toddy is Malaysia’s other palm wine. A drink once maligned, but now quietly making its way back into the spotlight. When you think of locally derived alcoholic beverages, it’s likely tuak that comes to mind. But I’m here to talk about a different kind of palm wine today, one that doesn’t get as much love, owing perhaps to its coloured past. The drink I’m referring to is, of course, toddy—a traditional fermented beverage that comes from the sap of coconut palms. The history of toddy here first began in the late 19th century, when Tamil and Keralan labourers tapped coconut palms for sap, which naturally fermented overnight into toddy. A cooling and mildly alcoholic drink, it served as a gentle relief at the end of the long, arduous days that characterised life on the plantation. However, the drink’s reputation took a sour turn at the hands of the British colonial authorities of the day, who associated toddy with disease and disorder, as well as the ‘unclean’ habits of the Indian migrant workers. A worker wheels bottles of freshly collected toddy This stigmatisation led to a burgeoning anti-toddy movement in the early 1900s. By the mid-1940s, toddy’s reputation was all but in the gutter, with restrictions enforced on its availability and consumption. Now, in the present day, interest is beginning to grow in the once-maligned beverage. Time has put enough distance between toddy and its sordid past to allow it to be appreciated in its own right. Tapped from the flower buds of coconut palms, the sweet sap that eventually becomes toddy is traditionally collected in clay pots. Beginning life as a sweet and lightly fizzy liquid, young toddy starts off low in alcohol but undergoes fermentation quickly. If left to its own devices, the drink develops a cloudier consistency, as well as more of a pronounced tang and an increased alcohol percentage, staying fresh for up to three days when refrigerated. Toddy comes from the sap of coconut palms Today, toddy is beginning to inch its way into modern mixology, with bars like Reka:Bar working it into their offerings. Places like Chelliah Toppu, established in the 1980s by the famed ‘Toddy Queen’, still serve the beverage as it was enjoyed all those decades ago, alongside flavourful peratal curries to which its tangy sweetness proves to be the perfect accompaniment. The journey of toddy, from its colonial-era stigma to its current resurgence, in many ways mirrors Malaysia’s evolving relationship with its own identity. It serves as a reminder that something once dismissed can be rediscovered and appreciated anew. Whether enjoyed in its traditional form, served in a rustic clay pot, or reimagined in a modern cocktail, toddy more than has its place in Malaysia’s beverage scene. Cast aside for far too long, it’s time we raised a glass to a drink that lifted many a weathered spirit in its day, and finally accorded it the recognition it deserves.
